text: Welcome to this robotic intuitive fourth generation left upper lobectomy with wedge of lower lobe.
My name is Ben Fleet.
I'll be editing this video.
The two surgeons are Mr. Ben Waterhouse as console and Mr. Joel Dunning as the assistant and proctor.
So we're starting here with four instrument arms.
We have the camera in arm two, a cadia in one, a Maryland bipolar in three, and finally a tip-up fenestrated in arm number four.
So now we're just showing the endocyanin green that was injected into a tiny nodule in the lower lobe.
So although the main tumor was in the upper lobe, as we were in there, there was also a secondary tiny three millimeter nodule in the lower lobe.
And the patient was sent to the CT scanner and rejected with 0.3 mils of ICG and also methylene blue, which is why you can see the blue stain there as well.
And now we're just starting the dissection of the inferior pulmonary ligament.
So just for the orientation, the diaphragm can be seen at the bottom of the screen, the pericardium to the left, and then to the right is the aorta.
And again, just slowly dissecting through that inferior pulmonary ligament.
So now we can see an L9 lymph node that is just being grafted there by the CADeR and the assistant has the sucker in the chest as well.
So overall there are five ports, the four instrument ports as mentioned earlier and also a low anterior assistant port as well.
The tumour, wedge and all lymph nodes will be taken out from the assistant port.
So now just removing that L9 lymph node and if possible, when removing the lymph nodes, it's best to try and take out the whole thing rather than in little pieces.
And then just handing that there to the assistant who then takes it out with a five millimeter Johan.
So we have a nice view there of the aorta on the right hand side and just come up to the inferior pulmonary vein.
So just pushing the lung forwards and then starting to dissect the subcarinal lymph nodes.
And the video has just been slightly sped up now.
And again, just do those station seven or subcarinal lymph nodes.
So we have the vagus and the esophagus there to the right-hand side.
And we're pushing the hilum forwards as we are dissecting to get that station seven lymph node.
As you can see there in the cadia, it's holding the station seven subcarina lymph node.
And again, as with the other lymph nodes, we're just trying to take this out in one piece.
And again, the lymph node just being taken out there by the Johan.
So you may have seen some of the little blue triangles pop up and that's because we have got a dual console set up.
So there is a person sat on the second console who is able to point and ask questions all as the operation is going on.
So now dissecting an L10 lymph node just off of the left main bronchus and pulmonary artery.
So just dissecting off the back of the pulmonary artery with the bronchus just below.
And again, this is the L10 lymph node.
And once again, we're trying to take this all in one piece.
So again, just having a look at that ICG labeled lower lobe nodule.
And this actually turned out to be benign in histology.
And this is just going to be wedged out before we proceed to the main left upper lobectomy.
This is the SureForm 45 in Arm 1 and this is a second generation stapler from Intuitive.
The staple options are white for vascular, blue for thin tissue, green for bronchus and black for heavy lung tissue.
As this is quite a thin wedge resection, we selected the blue staple.
So again this is the shore form 45 with a curved tip.
So the 45 is best for around vessels and the curved tip is also good for vessels.
And as this is quite a thin resection, both the 45 and the curved tip work quite well.
So just using three firings there to wedge it nicely.
And again, you can see that methylene blue stain in this section.
And they're just taking out that resected section.
So now that the wedge resection has been completed, we can continue with the subcarinal station, just making sure that it is completely empty.
So now just having a look slightly deeper, just to see if there's any more subcarinal lymph nodes that are present, and just making sure that we get it all out.
And there are just a few remnants of the subcarinal lymph nodes being brought out.
So for this we don't do lymph node sampling, instead we do the full lymph node dissection.
and again just taken out there.

So continuing underneath the left main bronchus, as you can see the left main bronchus above the cadia, below is the inferior pulmonary vein, and again just completely emptying out that subcarinal station of all its lymph nodes.
Just take out another lymph node there.
So now going above the pulmonary artery, which is just below the Johan instrument, and just finding some more of those L10 lymph nodes.
And again, just removing that as one whole lymph node.
So again, this is an L10 lymph node that's just coming out with the Johan there.
We've now moved the lung posteriorly and have come to the front of the hilum and you can see the phrenic nerve there just underneath the cadia going from top to bottom on the left hand side.
It's now just dissecting the lymph nodes over the superior pulmonary vein.
So these are more of those L10 lymph nodes but from the anterior hilum over the superior pulmonary vein.
So with the third arm now retracting the lung posteriorly, so we can get a good view of the anterior hilum.
And again, the video's been slightly sped up and just taking out some more of those lymph nodes.
My camera's just panned back a little bit now, just having a slightly better view of the superior pulmonary vein.
So with both the assistant and the third arm retracting, we're now going to start dissecting underneath the superior pulmonary vein and to remove any lymph nodes as we come to them.

So just lifting up the superior pulmonary vein there, and it's a really good idea to remove all of the lymph nodes as this reveals the underlying anatomy really well, as well as being good for the staging and the treatment for lung cancer.
So now just removing an L11 interlobar lymph node, and a map of the inter-lymph node stations can be found from the IASLC lymph node map, which can be found online.
So just in the fissure now, and I set out the pulmonary artery in the fissure.
This is the posterior oblique fissure, as you can see quite clearly.
So just a few more bits of the L10 lymph nodes from the hilum to clean out, and again just taking it out with the Johan there.
This is a great view now of the pulmonary artery, and that there is another lymph node, so that's an L11 lymph node.
And again, just removing that out now so that we can see the pulmonary artery branches.
And you can see that by taking out those lymph nodes, you really expose the branches of the pulmonary artery behind.
So now just putting a sling around the anterior oblique fissure, because we prefer to staple the lung tissue rather than using diathermy or dissection.
And as this is quite thin, we chose to use a white SureForm 45 to divide the lung tissue.
And this gives us a great view of the pulmonary artery behind, so that it's nice and safe for that final upper lobectomy.
And again, just removing a few more of those lymph nodes.
I can see a lymph node there being grasped by the cadia.
And that's been taken off the basal pulmonary artery, which goes to the left lower lobe.
So now dissecting between the lingular artery and the upper lobe bronchus and you can see the sucker just poking the bronchus and then just removing further L11 interlobar lymph nodes.
So now a great view just dissecting around the posterior segmental pulmonary artery to the upper lobe and in the foreground there is a very large lingular branch to the left upper lobe.
And the lymph node that's coming out now is a station 12 lobar lymph node.
And again, as with the others, it's taken out in full for the optimal cancer treatment and also to give the best vision possible.

So now we've actually gone around the lingula and the posterior segmental, and they looked ideally lined up, so both can be stapled with a sure form 45, the lingula first and the posterior segmental second.
So now we've gone over the top of the lung, looking at the hilum and the arch of the aorta to look for station 5 lymph nodes, so the station 5 subaortic lymph nodes.
So we've now done those lymph nodes and we're now going round the superior pulmonary vein, ready for a firing through on arm number 3.
So we have two 12mm ports in arms 1 and arm 3.
These are able to take the staplers.
And we also have two 8mm ports in arms 2 and 4, which don't need staplers.
So the staplers are 12mm, all other instruments are 8mm, including the camera.
And now just another lymph node coming off there, so probably an L12 lobe.
And now we're just seeing an anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch to the left upper lobe.
And we're just putting a sling around it from the front of the hilum to the back of the hilum.
So just for safety, so we know exactly where it is that we're stapling, as these are very large vessels.
If this was done incorrectly, there could be a lot of bleeding.
And again, just attaching that sling there.
So again, just using a Shore Form 45 through Portal 1.
So this is just cutting through the anterior segmental.
So we've currently done a lingula, a posterior, and the anterior segmental arteries.
So now back with the cadia.
I was lifting up with this sling.
There is a smaller surprise apical segmental artery
And as you can see, again, just using that SureForm 45 just to staple off the apical artery.
So in the end, we did have individual staplings of the anterior, apical, posterior and lingula, which is actually the classic configuration of the arteries to the left upper lobe.
And finally, just to finish off the lobectomy, using the green Sureform 45 from Arm 1.
We're choosing not to inflate the lung for this, so it can be inflated if we're unsure which is the upper lobe, but for this we were sure.
And then just cutting through that.
So that is a completed left upper lobectomy.
We've already done the lymphadenectomy.
So all that's left to do now is to remove the sample and check for hemostasis and then we are done.
So just putting a little swab in there again just to check for hemostasis.
Now just getting the endo bag.
So this is a nice cotton endo bag, which is nice and strong and is preferred over the plastic endo bag.
And it means that you can pull it nice and firmly.
And this is going to come out through that assistant port, which is very low and anterior in the eighth and ninth space.
And then here's a final view inside of the thoracic cavity.
